  "title_inferred": "Beimu (Fritillaria / Fritillary Bulb) — Names, Descriptions, Flavors, Indications, and Elucidations",
  "original_text": "【釋名】（《爾雅》，音萌）、勤母（《別錄》）、苦菜（《別錄》）、苦花（《別錄》）、空草（《本經》）、藥實。弘景曰：形似聚貝子，故名貝母。時珍曰：《詩》云言采其蝱，即此。一作虻，謂根狀如虻也。苦菜、藥實，與野苦蕒、黃藥子同名。

【集解】《別錄》曰：貝母，生晉地，十月採根，曝乾。恭曰：其葉似大蒜。四月蒜熟時采之，良。若十月，苗枯，根亦不佳也。出潤州、荊州、襄州者，最佳；江南諸州亦有。

頌曰：今河中、江陵府、郢、壽、隨、鄭、蔡、潤、滁州皆有之。二月生苗，莖細，青色。葉亦青，似蕎麥葉，隨苗出。七月開花，碧綠色，形如鼓子花。八月採根，根有瓣子，黃白色，如聚貝子。此有數種。陸璣《詩疏》云：蝱，貝母也。葉如栝蔞而細小。其子在根下，如芋子，正白，四方連累相著，有分解。今近道出者正類此。郭璞注《爾雅》言：白花葉似韭，此種罕復見之。曰：貝母中有獨顆團不作兩片無皺者，號曰丹龍精，不入藥用。誤服

根

【氣味】辛，平，無毒。《別錄》曰：苦，微寒。恭曰：味甘、苦，不辛。之才曰：濃朴、白薇為之使，惡桃花，畏秦艽、莽草、礜石，反烏頭。

【主治】傷寒煩熱，淋瀝邪氣，疝瘕，喉痺乳難，金瘡風痙（《本經》）。療腹中結實，心下滿，洗洗惡風寒，目眩項直，咳嗽上氣，止煩熱渴，出汗，安五臟，利骨髓（《別錄》）。服之不飢斷谷（弘景）。消痰，潤心肺。末和沙糖丸含，止嗽。燒灰油調，敷人畜惡瘡，斂瘡口（大明）。主胸脅逆氣，時疾黃疸。研末點目，去膚翳。以七枚作末酒服，治產難及胞衣不下。

【發明】承曰：貝母能散心胸郁結之氣，故《詩》云言：采其蝱，是也。作詩者，本以不得志而言。今用治心中氣不快、多愁郁者，殊有功。信矣。好古曰：貝母乃肺經氣分藥也。仲景治寒實結胸、外無熱証者，三物小陷胸湯主之，白散亦可，以其內有貝母也。成無己云：辛散而苦泄，桔梗、貝母之苦辛，用以下氣。機曰：俗以半夏有毒，用貝母代之。夫貝母乃太陰肺經之藥，半夏乃太陰脾經、陽明胃經之藥，何可以代？若虛勞咳嗽、吐血咯血、肺痿肺癰、婦人乳癰、癰疽及諸郁之証，半夏乃禁忌，皆貝母為向導，猶可代也；至於脾胃濕熱，涎化為痰，久則生火，痰火上攻，昏憒僵仆蹇澀諸証，生死旦夕，亦豈貝母可代乎？頌曰：貝母治惡瘡。唐人記其事云：江左嘗有商人，左膊上有瘡如人面，亦無他苦。商人戲以酒滴口中，其面赤色。以物食之，亦能食，多則膊內肉脹起。或不食，則一臂痺焉。有名醫教其歷試諸藥，金石草木之類，悉無所苦，至貝母，其瘡乃聚眉閉口。商人喜，因以小葦筒",
  "translation": "**[Explanation of Names]**
Also known as: 蝱 [méng] (from the *Erya* [classical glossary]; pronounced 'méng'), Qínmǔ [Diligent Mother] (from the *Bielu* [Supplementary Records]), Kǔcài [Bitter Vegetable] (from the *Bielu*), Kǔhuā [Bitter Flower] (from the *Bielu*), Kōngcǎo [Hollow Herb] (from the *Benjing* [Divine Husbandman's Classic of Materia Medica]), and Yàoshí [Drug Fruit].

Hongjing [Tao Hongjing] states: Its form resembles a cluster of cowrie shells [bèizi], hence the name Bèimǔ [Shell Mother]. Shizhen [Li Shizhen] states: The *Book of Odes* (*Shijing*) says 'going out to gather méng'—this refers to the same plant. It is also written as 虻 (méng, gadfly), meaning the root resembles a gadfly in shape. The names Kǔcài [Bitter Vegetable] and Yàoshí [Drug Fruit] are shared wit

results.title_inferred: Shanci Gu (Mountain Corm / Pleione / Cremastra) — Herb Entry
results.original_text:
【釋名】金燈（《拾遺》）、鬼燈檠（《綱目》）、朱姑（《綱目》）、鹿蹄草（《綱目》）、無義草。時珍曰：根狀如水慈菇，花狀如燈籠而朱色，故有諸名。段成式《酉陽雜俎》云：金燈之花與葉不相見，人惡種之，謂之無義草。又有試劍草，亦名鹿蹄草，與此同名，見後草之五。

【集解】藏器曰：山慈菇生山中濕地，葉似車前，根如慈菇。大明曰：零陵間有一種團慈菇，根如小蒜，所主略同。時珍曰：山慈菇處處有之。冬月生葉，如水仙花之葉而狹。二月中抽一莖，如箭杆，高尺許。莖端開花白色，亦有紅色、黃色者，上有黑點，其花乃衆花簇成一朵，如絲紐成可愛。三月結子，有三棱。四月初苗枯，即掘取其根，狀如慈菇及小蒜，遲則苗腐難尋矣。根苗與老鴉蒜極相類，但老鴉根無毛，慈菇有毛殼包裹爲異爾。用之，去毛殼。

根

【氣味】甘、微辛，有小毒。

【主治】癰腫瘡瘍、瘰癧結核等，醋磨敷之。亦剝人面皮，除斑（藏器）。主疔腫，攻毒破皮，解諸毒蠱毒，蛇蟲狂犬傷（時珍）。瘡腫，入蜜搗塗瘡口，候清血出，效（慎微）。塗乳癰、便毒，尤妙（時珍）。小便血淋澀痛，同地柏花陰乾，每用三錢，水煎服（《聖惠》）。
results.translation:
**[Names and Nomenclature]**
Alternate names: Jīndēng [Golden Lamp] (from *Shiyi* [Supplement to Materia Medica]), Guǐdēngqíng [Ghost Lamp Stand] (from *Gangmu*), Zhūgū [Red Corm] (from *Gangmu*), Lùtícǎo [Deer's Hoof Herb] (from *Gangmu*), and Wúyìcǎo [Faithless Herb].

Li Shizhen [the author] remarks: The root resembles that of the water caltrop [*Sagittaria*, i.e., water-arrowhead corm], and the flower resembles a lantern and is vermillion in color — hence all these names. Duan Chengshi, in his *Youyang Zazu* [Miscellany of Youyang], writes: "The flower of Jīndēng and its leaves never appear at the same time; people dislike growing it and call it Wúyìcǎo [Faithless Herb]." There is also a plant called Shìjiàncǎo [Sword-Testing Herb], likewise named Lùtícǎo, which shares this alternate name — see later in Chapter Five of the Herb section.

**[Collected Explanations]**
Chen Cangqi [Tang-dynasty compiler of *Bencao Shiyi*] states: Mountain corm [Shāncígū] grows in damp mountain terrain; its leaves resemble those of plantain [*Plantago*], and its root resembles that of the arrowhead corm.

Da Ming [another cited authority] states: In the Lingling region there is a variety called tuán-cígū [round corm], whose root resembles a small garlic bulb; its medicinal uses are broadly similar.

Li Shizhen states: Shāncígū is found everywhere. It puts forth leaves in winter, resembling the leaves of the narcissus [*Narcissus tazetta*] but narrower. In the second month [approximately March], a single stalk is sent up, resembling an arrow shaft, reaching about one *chi* [roughly 30 cm] in height. At the tip of the stalk it bears flowers that are white — though some are red or yellow — marked with black spots. The flower is made up of many smaller florets clustered into a single head, prettily twisted together like braided threads. Seeds are set in the third month [April] and have three ridges. In the early fourth month [May] the seedlings wither; one should dig up the root at that time, as it resembles an arrowhead corm or a small garlic bulb. If one delays, the seedlings rot and become difficult to locate. The root and seedling closely resemble those of lǎoyāsuàn [crow garlic / *Lycoris radiata*], but the roots of crow garlic have no fibrous covering, while the mountain corm root is enclosed in a hairy husk — this is the distinguishing feature. When preparing it for use, remove the hairy husk.

**Root**

**[Qi and Flavor]**
Sweet, slightly acrid [pungent]; mildly toxic.

**[Principal Indications and Treatments]**
- For carbuncles, swellings, sores [*yōng zhǒng chuāng yáng*], and scrofulous lumps and nodules [*luǒlì jiéhé*]: grind with vinegar and apply topically. It also exfoliates facial skin and removes blemishes [*bān*]. (Chen Cangqi)
- For boils [*dīng zhǒng*]: attacks toxins and breaks through the skin; resolves all manner of poisons including *gu* toxin [parasitic/magical poisoning]; [effective for] snakebite, insect bite, and mad-dog [rabid animal] wounds. (Li Shizhen)
- For sores and swellings: pound with honey and apply to the mouth of the wound; wait until clear blood emerges — effective. (Tang Shenwei [*Zhenghe Bencao* compiler, attributed as Shenwei/慎微])
- Applied to breast abscesses [*rǔyōng*] and venereal sores [*biàndú*]: especially effective. (Li Shizhen)
- For bloody painful dribbling urination [*xiǎobiàn xuè lín sè tòng*]: combine with shade-dried *Selaginella* flowers [*dìbǎihuā*]; use three *qian* per dose, decocted in water and taken orally. (From *Shenghui Fang* [Prescriptions for Sacred Relief])

entities_mentioned.name: 山慈菇 (Shāncígū)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cremastra appendiculata or Pleione bulbocodioides (family Orchidaceae); possibly also Tulipa edulis — the term covers several corm-bearing plants
entities_mentioned.notes: The description of the flower as many florets clustered into one head with black spots, with a three-ridged seed capsule, is consistent with Cremastra appendiculata. However, 'Shāncígū' is a collective name historically applied to multiple species including Pleione spp. and occasionally Tulipa edulis.

entities_mentioned.name: 金燈 (Jīndēng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lycoris aurea or Lycoris radiata (family Amaryllidaceae) — alternate identification in some sources; here used as alternate name for Shāncígū
entities_mentioned.notes: In this text, Jīndēng is treated as a synonym for Shāncígū, but the name is also used independently for Lycoris species. Context here suggests it refers to the same Cremastra/Pleione plant.

entities_mentioned.name: 老鴉蒜 (Lǎoyāsuàn)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lycoris radiata (red spider lily, family Amaryllidaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a comparative species to distinguish from Shāncígū; the text notes that Lycoris roots lack the fibrous hairy covering present on Shāncígū.

entities_mentioned.name: 地柏花 (Dìbǎihuā)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Selaginella tamariscina or related Selaginella species (spikemoss, family Selaginellaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in combination with Shāncígū root for bloody dysuria; shade-dried before use.

entities_mentioned.name: 水慈菇 (Shuǐcígū)
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced only as a morphological comparator for the root shape of Shāncígū.

entities_mentioned.name: 車前 (Chēqián)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Plantago asiatica or Plantago major (plantain, family Plantaginaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced as a leaf-shape comparator by Chen Cangqi.

results.translator_notes: 1. The character in '癰腫瘡癧' (OCR rendering '癧') most likely represents 瘍 (yáng), meaning 'sores/ulcers,' giving the compound 瘡瘍 — this has been silently corrected in the cleaned text. 2. The OCR rendering 'luǒ癧' uses a partially corrupted character for 瘰 (luǒ) in 瘰癧 (scrofula); corrected silently. 3. 'Gudu' (蠱毒) refers to a category of poisoning in classical Chinese medicine that includes both toxic parasites and, in some traditions, magical/malevolent poisoning — translated contextually as 'gu toxin.' 4. '便毒' (biàndú) is rendered as 'venereal sores,' its standard classical meaning referring to inguinal bubo or chancre-like lesions in the groin. 5. The text cites Duan Chengshi's *Youyang Zazu* (酉陽雜俎), a Tang-dynasty miscellany, as a literary source — this is a genuine citation and not a medical text. 6. The reference to 'Shenwei' (慎微) points to Tang Shenwei, compiler of the *Jingshi Zhenglei Bencao* (經史證類本草).

results.title_inferred: Shi Suan (Stone Garlic / Red Spider Lily) — Root Properties and Medical Uses
results.original_text:
【釋名】烏蒜（《綱目》）、老鴉蒜（《救荒》）、蒜頭草（《綱目》）、婆婆酸（《綱目》）、一枝箭

【集解】頌曰：水麻生鼎州、黔州，其根名石蒜，九月採之。或云金燈花根，亦名石蒜，即此類也。時珍曰：石蒜，處處下濕地有之，古謂之烏蒜，俗謂之老鴉蒜、一枝箭是也。春初生葉，如蒜秧及山慈菇葉，背有劍脊，四散布地。七月苗枯，乃於平地抽出一莖如箭杆，長尺許。莖端開花四、五朵，六出紅色，如山丹花狀而瓣長，黃蕊長須。其根狀如蒜，皮色紫赤，肉白色。此有小毒，而《救荒本草》言其可炸熟水浸過食，蓋爲救荒爾。一種葉如大韭，四、五月抽莖，開花如小萱花黃白色者，謂之鐵色箭，功與此同。二物並抽莖開花，後乃生葉，葉、花不相見，與金燈同。

根

【氣味】辛、甘，溫，有小毒。

【主治】敷貼腫毒（蘇頌）。疔瘡惡核，可水煎服取汗，及搗敷之。又中溪毒者，酒煎半升服，取吐良（時珍）。
results.translation:
**Names:** Wu Suan [Black Garlic] (Bencao Gangmu); Lao Ya Suan [Crow's Garlic] (Jiuhuang Bencao); Suan Tou Cao [Garlic-head Herb] (Bencao Gangmu); Popo Suan [Old Woman's Sour] (Bencao Gangmu); Yi Zhi Jian [Single-shaft Arrow] (implied by text).

**Collected Explanations:** Su Song states: Shui Ma [Water Hemp] grows in Dingzhou and Qianzhou; its root is called Shi Suan [Stone Garlic] and is harvested in the ninth month. Some say it is the root of Jin Deng Hua [Golden Lantern Flower], also known as Shi Suan — these are of the same kind. Li Shizhen states: Shi Suan is found everywhere in low-lying damp ground. In ancient times it was called Wu Suan [Black Garlic]; in common usage it is called Lao Ya Suan [Crow's Garlic] or Yi Zhi Jian [Single-shaft Arrow]. In early spring it sends up leaves resembling garlic shoots or the leaves of Shan Ci Gu [Pleione / mountain tuber orchid], with a sword-like ridge along the back, spreading out flat across the ground in all directions. By the seventh month the foliage has withered, whereupon a single stem, resembling an arrow shaft and about a foot in length, rises straight from level ground. At the tip of the stem it bears four or five flowers of six petals, red in colour, resembling the flowers of Shan Dan [Mountain Lily] but with longer petals and long yellow stamens. The root is shaped like garlic, with a purplish-red outer skin and white flesh. It has mild toxicity; however, the Jiuhuang Bencao [Famine Relief Materia Medica] states it may be eaten after deep-frying and soaking in water — this being a measure adopted only in times of famine. There is a related variety whose leaves resemble large chives; in the fourth and fifth months it puts up a stem and bears small yellow-white flowers resembling those of Xuan Hua [daylily], called Tie Se Jian [Iron-coloured Arrow]. Its properties and functions are the same as those of Shi Suan. Both plants produce stems and flowers before the leaves emerge; leaf and flower are never seen together — as is also the case with Jin Deng [Golden Lantern].

**Root**

**Qi and Flavour:** Acrid and sweet; warm; mildly toxic.

**Principal Indications and Treatments:**
- Applied externally as a poultice for swellings and toxic sores (Su Song).
- For deep-rooted sores [ding chuang] and malignant hard nodules [e he]: decoct in water, take orally to induce sweating; also pound fresh root and apply as a poultice externally.
- For poisoning from mountain stream water [xi du]: decoct in half a sheng [approx. 0.5 litre] of wine, take orally to induce vomiting — this is an effective treatment (Li Shizhen).

entities_mentioned.name: 石蒜 (Shi Suan)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lycoris radiata (Red Spider Lily, family Amaryllidaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The description — red six-petaled flowers on a leafless scape arising in summer/autumn, strap-like spring leaves withering before flowering, bulb with purple-red skin and white flesh — is a precise match for Lycoris radiata.

entities_mentioned.name: 鐵色箭 (Tie Se Jian)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lycoris aurea (Golden Spider Lily) or possibly Lycoris longituba
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as having leaves like large chives, yellow-white flowers resembling small daylilies, blooming in the fourth-fifth months. Likely another Lycoris species; L. aurea is a plausible candidate given the yellow-white flower description.

entities_mentioned.name: 金燈花 (Jin Deng Hua)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lycoris radiata or a closely related Lycoris sp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned as a synonym or close relative; shares the characteristic of leaf and flower never appearing simultaneously.

entities_mentioned.name: 山慈菇 (Shan Ci Gu)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pleione bulbocodioides or Iphigenia indica (various authors differ)
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced only for leaf comparison; not the subject of this entry.

entities_mentioned.name: 山丹 (Shan Dan)
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced only for flower-shape comparison.

results.translator_notes: 1. The entry's opening list of synonyms appears to have been truncated in the source (ending mid-list after 'Yi'); the text as supplied likely originally continued with '一枝箭' as a fifth synonym, which is confirmed later in the Collected Explanations section — this has been silently restored in the cleaned text. 2. 'Xi du' (溪毒) refers to a category of environmental poisoning thought to be contracted from miasmatic or contaminated mountain stream water, a recognised disease concept in classical Chinese medicine. 3. The Jiuhuang Bencao (救荒本草) reference indicates the plant was used as famine food despite its mild toxicity, a practice the author Li Shizhen contextualises as an emergency measure only. 4. 'Half a sheng' (半升) is rendered approximately; Ming-dynasty liquid measure places 1 sheng at roughly 1 litre, so half a sheng ≈ 500 ml.

results.original_text:
【釋名】金盞銀台。時珍曰：此物宜卑濕處，不可缺水，故名水仙。金盞銀台，花之狀也。

【集解】機曰：水仙花葉似蒜，其花香甚清。九月初栽於肥壤，則花茂盛，瘦地則無花。五月初收根，以童尿浸一宿，晒乾，懸火暖處。若不移宿根更旺。時珍曰：水仙叢生下濕處。其根似蒜及薤而長，外有赤皮裹之。冬月生葉，似薤及蒜。春初抽莖，如蔥頭。莖頭開花數朵，大如簪頭，狀如酒杯，五尖上承，黃心，宛然盞樣，其花瑩韻，其香清幽。一種千葉者，花皺，下輕黃而上淡白，不作杯狀，人重之，指爲真水仙，蓋不然，乃一物二種爾。亦有紅花者。按：段成式《酉陽雜俎》云：捺祗出拂林國，根大如雞卵，苗長三四尺，葉似蒜葉，中心抽條，莖端開花，六出紅白色，花心黃赤，不結子，冬生夏死。取花壓油，塗身去風氣。據此形狀，與水仙仿佛，豈外國名謂不同耶？

根

【氣味】苦、微辛，滑，寒，無毒。土宿真君曰：取汁伏汞，煮雄黃，拒火。

【主治】花
作香澤，塗身理發，去風氣。又療婦人五心發熱，同乾荷葉、赤芍藥等分，爲末，白湯每服二錢，熱自退也（時珍，出《衛生易簡方》）。
results.translation:
**[Names and Appellations]** Also known as 'Golden Cup, Silver Terrace' [金盞銀台]. Li Shizhen states: This plant thrives in low-lying, moist places and cannot do without water; hence its name 'Water Immortal' [水仙, Shuixian]. 'Golden Cup, Silver Terrace' describes the appearance of the flower.

**[Collected Explanations]** [Wang] Ji states: The leaves of the Water Narcissus resemble those of garlic, and its flowers have an exceptionally clear fragrance. If planted in rich soil at the beginning of the ninth month, the flowers will bloom abundantly; in poor soil, it will not flower at all. At the beginning of the fifth month, collect the roots, soak them overnight in boys' urine [童尿], dry them in the sun, and hang them in a warm place near a fire. If the roots are not transplanted, the established roots will grow even more vigorously.

Li Shizhen states: The Water Narcissus grows in clusters in low, damp places. Its roots resemble those of garlic and Chinese scallion [薤, Allium chinense] but are longer, with a reddish skin encasing them. In winter it sends out leaves resembling those of Chinese scallion and garlic. In early spring it puts forth a stem like that of a scallion head. At the top of the stem several flowers open; they are as large as the head of a hairpin, shaped like a wine cup, borne on five pointed petals, with a yellow centre that truly resembles a small cup [盞]. The flowers are lustrous and graceful, their fragrance clear and subtle. There is a double-flowered [千葉, 'thousand-petalled'] variety whose flowers are ruffled, pale yellow below and light white above, and do not form a cup shape. People prize this variety and call it the 'true' Water Narcissus, but this is incorrect — the two are merely two forms of the same plant. There is also a red-flowered variety.

Note: Duan Chengshi's *Youyang Zazu* [酉陽雜俎] records: 'Naqiha [捺祗] comes from the kingdom of Fulin [拂林, i.e., Byzantium/Rome]. Its root is as large as a hen's egg; the sprout grows three to four feet tall; the leaves resemble garlic leaves; from the centre a stalk rises, and flowers open at the tip of the stem — six-petalled, in red and white, with a yellow-red flower centre; it bears no seeds; it grows in winter and dies in summer. The flowers are pressed to extract oil, which is applied to the body to dispel wind-qi [風氣].' Judging by this description, it closely resembles the Water Narcissus — could it simply be that the foreign name differs?

**Root**

**[Flavour and Nature]** Bitter, slightly acrid, slippery [滑], cold, non-toxic. The Venerable Tuxu [土宿真君] states: The juice can be used to subdue mercury [伏汞], to treat [i.e., react with] realgar [雄黃] when boiled, and to resist fire. [The indications for the root are] lacking [ILLEGIBLE: the main-treatment entry for the root appears to have been omitted in the source].

**[Indications] — Flower**
Prepared as a fragrant emollient [香澤], applied to the body and used to dress the hair, it dispels wind-qi. It also treats heat effusion in a woman's five centres [五心發熱, i.e., heat in the palms, soles, and chest]. Combined in equal parts with dried lotus leaf [乾荷葉] and red peony root [赤芍藥], the mixture is ground into a powder; take two qian [錢] per dose in plain hot water — the heat will subside of itself. (Li Shizhen, citing the *Weisheng Yijian Fang* [衛生易簡方].)

entities_mentioned.name: 水仙 (Shuixian) / 金盞銀台
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Narcissus tazetta (Chinese sacred lily / polyanthus narcissus)
entities_mentioned.notes: The description of cup-shaped flowers with a yellow corona, strap-like leaves resembling garlic, winter-growing bulbs, and clear fragrance is a strong match for Narcissus tazetta or a closely related species. The 'thousand-petalled' form likely corresponds to the double-flowered cultivar.

entities_mentioned.name: 捺祗 (Naqiha) from 拂林 (Fulin)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Narcissus tazetta or a related Narcissus species; Fulin is commonly identified as the Byzantine/Eastern Roman Empire or Syria
entities_mentioned.notes: Li Shizhen speculates this foreign plant may be the same as Shuixian under a different name. The six-petalled red-and-white flower with yellow-red centre and winter growth cycle are consistent with a Narcissus, though the colour description could suggest another Amaryllidaceae species.

entities_mentioned.name: 赤芍藥 (red peony root)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Paeonia lactiflora or Paeonia veitchii, root

entities_mentioned.name: 雄黃 (realgar)
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned in the context of an alchemical property of the narcissus root juice.

results.translator_notes:
1. The main-treatment entry for the Root (根【主治】) appears to have been entirely omitted in the source text — the section breaks directly from the flavour note to the Flower indications. This is flagged as a probable lacuna rather than an OCR error.
2. 'Boys' urine' (童尿) was a standard classical Chinese pharmaceutical solvent and preservative agent.
3. 'Five-centre heat' (五心發熱) is a classical TCM symptom pattern referring to a subjective sensation of heat in the palms of both hands, soles of both feet, and the chest/heart area — broadly corresponding to yin-deficiency heat in modern TCM.
4. The preparation method for the flower (ground powder taken with hot water at 2 qian per dose) is attributed by Li Shizhen to the *Weisheng Yijian Fang* (衛生易簡方), a Ming-dynasty popular medical formulary.
5. The alchemical note by 土宿真君 (Tuxu Zhenjun) refers to esoteric Daoist-alchemical uses and is not a medical indication proper.

【釋名】根名茹根（《本經》）、蘭根（《本經》）、地筋（《別錄》）。時珍曰：茅葉如矛，故謂之茅。其根牽連，故謂之茹。《易》曰：拔茅連茹，是也。有數種：夏花者，爲茅；秋
花者，爲菅，二物功用相近，而名謂不同。《詩》云：白華菅兮，白茅束兮，是也。《別錄》
不分茅、菅乃二種，謂茅根一名地菅，一名地筋，而有名未用又出地筋，一名菅根。蓋二物之根狀皆如筋，可通名地筋，不可並名菅也，正之。

【集解】《別錄》曰：茅根，生楚地山谷田野，六月採根。

弘景曰：此即今白茅菅，《詩》云：露彼菅茅，是也。其根如渣芹甜美。

頌曰：處處有之。春生芽，布地如針，俗謂之茅針，亦可啖，甚益小兒。夏生白花茸茸
然，至秋而枯。其根至潔白，六月採之。又有菅，亦茅類也。陸璣《草木疏》云：菅，似茅

苴之用，《本經》所用茅根是也。其根幹之，夜視有光，故腐則變爲螢火。菅茅只生山上，似白茅而長，

  "title_inferred": "White Cogon Grass (Baimaо / 白茅) — Root, Flower, and Roof Thatch",
  "original_text": "【釋名】根名茹根（《本經》）、蘭根（《本經》）、地筋（《別錄》）。時珍曰：茅葉如矛，故謂之茅。其根牽連，故謂之茹。《易》曰：拔茅連茹，是也。有數種：夏花者，爲茅；秋花者，爲菅，二物功用相近，而名謂不同。《詩》云：白華菅兮，白茅束兮，是也。《別錄》不分茅、菅乃二種，謂茅根一名地菅，一名地筋，而有名未用又出地筋，一名菅根。蓋二物之根狀皆如筋，可通名地筋，不可並名菅也，正之。

【集解】《別錄》曰：茅根，生楚地山谷田野，六月採根。

弘景曰：此即今白茅菅，《詩》云：露彼菅茅，是也。其根如渣芹甜美。

頌曰：處處有之。春生芽，布地如針，俗謂之茅針，亦可啖，甚益小兒。夏生白花茸茸然，至秋而枯。其根至潔白，六月採之。又有菅，亦茅類也。陸璣《草木疏》云：菅，似茅而滑無毛，根下五寸中有白粉者，柔韌宜爲索，漚之尤善。其未漚者名野菅，入藥與茅功等。

時珍曰：茅有白茅、菅茅、黃茅、香茅、芭茅數種，葉皆相似。白茅短小，三、四月開白花成穗，結細實。其根甚長，白軟如筋而有節，味甘，俗呼絲茅，可以苫蓋，及供祭祀苞苴之用，《本經》所用茅根是也。其根幹之，夜視有光，故腐則變爲螢火。菅茅只生山上，似白茅而長，入秋抽莖，開花成穗如荻花，結實尖黑，長分許，粘衣刺人。其根短硬如細竹根，無節而微甘，亦可入藥，功不及白茅，《爾雅》所謂白華野菅是也。黃茅似菅茅，而莖上開葉，莖下有白粉，根頭有黃毛，根亦短而細硬無節。秋深開花穗如菅，可爲索，古名黃菅，《別錄》所用菅根是也。香茅，一名菁茅，一名瓊茅，生湖南及江淮間，葉有三脊，其氣香芬，可以包藉及縮酒，禹貢所謂荊州苞匭菁茅是也。芭茅叢生，葉大如蒲，長六、七尺，有二種，即芒也。見後芒下。

茅根

【氣味】甘，寒，無毒。
甘，平，無毒。大明曰：涼。
甘，溫，無毒。
苦，平，無毒。

【主治】勞傷虛羸，補中益氣，除瘀血、血閉寒熱，利小便（《本經》）。下五淋，除客熱在腸胃，止渴堅筋，婦人崩中。久服利人（《別錄》）。主婦人月經不勻，通血脈淋瀝（大明）。止吐衄諸血，傷寒噦逆，肺熱喘急，水腫黃膽，解酒毒（時珍）。下水（《別錄》）。治消渴，能破血（甄權）。通小腸，治鼻衄及暴下血，水煮服之。惡瘡癰腫、軟癤未潰。

花
煎飲，止吐血衄血，並塞鼻。又敷灸瘡不合，刀箭金瘡，止血並痛（大明）。

屋上敗茅
猝吐血，銼三升，酒浸煮一升服。和醬汁研，敷斑瘡及蠶嚙瘡（藏器）。屋四角。

【發明】弘景曰：茅根，服食斷谷甚良。俗方稀用，惟煎汁療淋及崩中爾。時珍曰：白茅根甘，能除伏熱，利小便，故能止諸血噦逆、喘急消渴，治黃膽水腫，乃良物也。世人因微而忽之，惟事苦寒之劑，致傷沖和之氣，烏足知此哉？
時珍曰：按《陳文中小兒方》：治痘瘡潰爛，難靨不乾。多年牆屋上爛茅，擇洗焙乾，爲末摻之。此蓋取其性寒而解毒，又多受雨露霜雪之氣，兼能燥濕也。",
  "translation": "**[Explanation of Names]**
The root is called rúgēn [茹根] (in the *Shennong Bencao Jing* [*Classic of Materia Medica*]), lángēn [蘭根] (in the *Bencao Jing*), and dìjīn [地筋, "ground sinew"] (in the *Mingyi Bielu* [*Supplementary Records of Famous Physicians*]).

Li Shizhen states: The leaf of máo resembles a spear (máo [矛]), hence the name máo [茅]. Its roots are interconnected and trailing, hence the name rú [茹, "connected"]. The *Book of Changes* [*Yi*] says: "Pulling up cogon grass [máo], the roots come up connected [lián rú]"—this is exactly what is meant.
